islamic.text: Prophet Adam, peace be upon him, holds a revered position in Islam as the first human and the first prophet of Allah. His creation is detailed in the Holy Quran, where Allah fashioned him from clay, breathing into him a soul. Allah then commanded the angels to prostrate before Adam, recognizing his unique honor and the knowledge bestowed upon him. Iblis, however, refused out of arrogance, becoming Shaytan, the accursed one. Allah taught Adam the names of all things, showcasing his superior understanding. Adam was initially placed in Jannah (Paradise), and from his rib, Allah created Hawwa (Eve) to be his companion. Allah permitted them to enjoy all its fruits but warned them against approaching a specific tree. Shaytan, driven by envy, whispered temptations, leading Adam and Hawwa to partake of the forbidden fruit. Immediately recognizing their error, they repented sincerely, a supplication recorded in the Quran (7:23), and Allah mercifully forgave them. Subsequently, they were sent down to Earth, where they were tasked with populating the world and establishing monotheism. Adam diligently fulfilled his prophetic mission, guiding his progeny towards the worship of the One God and laying the foundations of human civilization. He lived a long life, fulfilling his role as Allah's representative on Earth before his passing.

christian.text: According to the Christian, specifically, Adam was the first human created by God. Formed from the dust of the ground, God breathed life into him, making him a living soul. God placed Adam in the Garden of Eden, entrusting him with its care and dominion over creation. Recognizing his need for a companion, God fashioned Eve from Adam’s rib, presenting her as his wife. They were commanded not to eat from the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. Tempted by a serpent, Eve ate the forbidden fruit and gave some to Adam, who also ate. This act of disobedience, known as the Fall, introduced sin and death into the world, separating humanity from God. As a consequence, they were expelled from Eden. Adam then toiled the ground and fathered children, including Cain, Abel, and Seth. He lived 930 years, becoming the patriarch of all humanity, whose sin necessitated God’s plan of salvation through Christ. His life marks the beginning of human history and the entry of sin into creation, as understood in Christian theology.

jewish.text: Adam, the foundational figure in Jewish tradition, is introduced in, primarily in. Created by God from the dust of the earth and infused with the breath of life, Adam was the first human being, uniquely made in the Divine image. God placed him in Gan Eden, the Garden of Eden, instructing him not to eat from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. Adam was given the profound task of naming all living creatures, a demonstration of his intellectual capacity and dominion. Recognizing his need for companionship, God fashioned Chava (Eve) from Adam's rib, creating the first couple. However, their blessed existence was tragically altered when Chava, persuaded by the serpent, ate from the forbidden tree and offered it to Adam, who also partook. This act of disobedience resulted in their expulsion from Gan Eden, introducing labor, pain, and mortality into the human condition. Despite this, Adam and Chava began humanity’s lineage, raising children such as Kayin (Cain), Hevel (Abel), and Shet (Seth). Adam lived a total of 930 years, his life chronicled in as a pivotal account of creation, divine instruction, human choice, and the enduring consequences that define the human experience in Jewish thought.

bahai.text: Adam, revered in the Bahá'í Faith as the first in a succession of Divine Messengers or Manifestations of God, heralded the dawn of a new spiritual cycle for humanity. His coming, as elucidated in Bahá'í writings, marked a crucial turning point, initiating humanity's collective spiritual and social development. Bahá'í teachings do not interpret the biblical account of Adam as the literal first man created on Earth; rather, humanity had existed for eons prior to his advent. Instead, Adam represents the first in a long line of Divine Teachers sent by God to educate and guide humankind. The narrative of the Garden of Eden and the "fall" is understood symbolically. The Garden signifies the world of God, or the divine realm, while Adam and Eve represent the collective soul of humanity. The "tree of good and evil" symbolizes the material world or self, and the "fall" denotes humanity's descent from a state of pure spiritual unity into the complexities of the material realm, accompanied by the gradual unfolding of human consciousness. Through Adam, humanity received its initial spiritual laws and guidance, establishing the foundation upon which subsequent Manifestations, such as Noah, Abraham, Moses, Zoroaster, Buddha, Christ, Muhammad, the Báb, and Bahá’u’lláh, would build. His mission was to awaken spiritual awareness and civilize a nascent humanity, setting the stage for progressive.

islamic.text: Prophet Idris, revered in Islam, is a significant figure mentioned in the Holy Quran, specifically in Surah Maryam (19:56-57) and Surah Al-Anbiya (21:85). The Quran describes him as a man of truth and a prophet, stating, "And mention in the Book Idris. Indeed, he was a man of truth and a prophet. And We raised him to a high station." Islamic tradition identifies Idris as one of the earliest prophets, living after Prophet Seth and before Prophet Nuh (Noah), carrying forth the message of pure monotheism. He is widely recognized for his profound wisdom, extensive knowledge, and unwavering devotion to Allah. Attributed to Idris are several pioneering advancements for humanity. He is often credited as the first to write with a pen, laying the foundational stone for recorded knowledge and the transmission of divine revelations. Furthermore, he is widely believed to have introduced the art of tailoring clothes, moving people away from rudimentary coverings and promoting a sense of dignity. Tradition also speaks of his deep understanding of astronomy, mathematics, and various sciences, which he used to educate and enlighten his community. Idris dedicated his life to teaching monotheism (Tawhid) and righteous conduct, guiding his people towards the worship of the One God and away from idolatry. His prophethood served as a beacon of light in an early era, emphasizing divine guidance, spiritual purification, and the pursuit of beneficial knowledge. The "high station" to which Allah raised him signifies his exalted spiritual status, profound honor, and closeness to God, a testament to his exemplary life and devotion.

christian.text: Enoch, an early patriarch in the lineage tracing from Adam to Noah, is a figure of profound faith within Christian scripture. in the Christian introduces him as the son of Jared and the father of Methuselah, noting his lifespan of 365 years. His most distinguishing characteristic, however, is the recurring statement that "Enoch walked with God." Unlike others in the genealogical record, declares that "he was not, for God took him." This unique departure from earthly life is further expounded upon in. states, "By faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death; and was not found, because God had translated him: for before his translation he had this testimony, that he pleased God." This passage underscores his extraordinary faith and the divine intervention that spared him the experience of death, transporting him directly into God's presence. Additionally, the Epistle of reveals Enoch as a prophet, foretelling the Lord's coming with "ten thousands of His saints, to execute judgment on all, to convict all who are ungodly among them of all their ungodly deeds which they have committed in an ungodly way, and of all the harsh things which ungodly sinners have spoken against Him." Enoch remains a testament to a life lived in intimate communion with God, culminating in a supernatural ascent.

He is understood to have lived a righteous existence, striving to uphold God's ways amidst an era increasingly marked by moral decline and sin. His unique spiritual journey set him apart, illustrating a continuous commitment to God's will. concludes his story with the enigmatic declaration, "and he was not, for God took him." Within Jewish tradition, this phrase has elicited various interpretations. Some understand it to signify a unique, divinely orchestrated pious death, where God removed him from the world before his time to prevent him from succumbing to the surrounding wickedness or to reward his exceptional righteousness. Others view it as a spiritual elevation, a removal from earthly existence due to his profound dedication. Regardless of the precise interpretation, Enoch's legacy remains that of a paragon of faith, a man whose life exemplified deep commitment to God, leading to an extraordinary divine intervention in his earthly tenure.

islamic.text: Prophet Nuh, revered in Islam, was a messenger sent by Allah to his people, who had strayed from monotheism into idol worship, worshipping deities like Wadd, Suwa', and Yaghuth. For an astonishing 950 years, as recounted in the Quran, Nuh tirelessly invited them back to the worship of the One God, warning them of divine punishment. Despite his persistent calls, his people stubbornly rejected his message, mocking him and demanding an end to his warnings. They refused to abandon their idols, leading Nuh to finally supplicate to Allah for assistance against their disbelief and transgression. In response, Allah commanded Nuh to construct a magnificent Ark, despite the ridicule of his people who saw no sea nearby. He was instructed to take aboard a pair of every living species, along with his believing family members, but excluding those who disbelieved, including one of his own sons and his wife. When the appointed time came, the heavens opened with torrential rain and water gushed forth from the earth, causing a devastating deluge that submerged everything. The Ark, by Allah's command, sailed safely through the towering waves, carrying Nuh and the believers to salvation. All who disbelieved perished in the flood, a monumental sign of Allah's justice. The Ark eventually rested upon Mount Judi, marking the end of the cataclysm and the beginning of a new era for humanity, with Prophet Nuh as a pivotal figure in its renewal, ensuring the continuation of human and animal life on earth.

christian.text: Noah, a righteous man blameless among his generation, walked faithfully with God at a time when human wickedness covered the earth. As recorded in the Christian, particularly, God observed the pervasive corruption and grieved that He had made mankind. Deciding to cleanse the earth with a great flood, God chose Noah and his family to preserve life. He commanded Noah to build a massive ark, providing precise dimensions and instructions for its construction. Noah diligently obeyed, gathering two of every kind of unclean animal and bird, and seven pairs of every clean animal and bird, along with his wife, his three sons—Shem, Ham, and Japheth—and their wives. When Noah was six hundred years old, the rains descended for forty days and forty nights, and the springs of the great deep burst forth, covering the highest mountains. All living creatures outside the ark perished. After 150 days, the waters receded, and the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. Noah sent out a raven and then doves, one of which returned with a fresh olive leaf, signaling that the land was drying. Upon God's command, Noah and his family emerged, and he built an altar to the Lord, offering sacrifices. God then made a covenant with Noah and all living creatures, promising never again to destroy the earth by flood, setting the rainbow in the sky as an everlasting sign of this promise. From Noah's sons, the earth was repopulated.

He, his wife, his three sons – Shem, Ham, and Japheth – and their wives, along with pairs of all living creatures, entered the ark. In response, God established an eternal covenant with Noah and all humanity, promising never again to destroy the world by flood, with the rainbow as its perpetual sign. Noah was also given the Seven Noahide Laws, a universal moral code binding on all mankind. Living 950 years, Noah became a patriarch of renewed civilization, through whom all subsequent generations descended.

bahai.text: Nuh, known as Noah in some traditions, is revered in the Baháʼí Faith as a Manifestation of God, a divinely appointed Messenger in the unbroken chain of Prophets. Nuh's primary mission was to warn his people, who had plunged into heedlessness and idolatry, urging them to turn towards the One True God. Despite his arduous efforts and numerous proofs, the vast majority rejected his divine call, mocking his message and refusing to heed his admonitions. Consequently, Nuh announced the approach of a great deluge. In Baháʼí understanding, this "flood" carried both a literal aspect, confined to a specific region, and a profound spiritual meaning. It symbolized the overwhelming spiritual darkness and corruption that engulfed the disbelievers, separating them from the light of God’s Cause. islamic.text: Prophet Hud, peace be upon him, was a noble messenger sent by Allah to the ancient Arab people of 'Ad. This powerful nation, known for their immense physical strength, impressive architectural feats, and material wealth, resided in the sandy plains of Al-Ahqaf. Despite Allah's blessings, they had strayed into idolatry, worshipping false gods, and were characterized by arrogance and tyranny. Hud compassionately called them to Tawhid, to worship Allah alone, reminding them of His sole sovereignty and bounties. He urged them to abandon their idols, repent for their sins, and seek forgiveness, warning of divine retribution if they persisted in their disbelief. However, the people of 'Ad stubbornly rejected Hud's clear message. They ridiculed him, accused him of falsehood, and dismissed his warnings, demanding that he bring forth the promised punishment if he was truthful. Despite Hud's persistent pleas and guidance, they remained defiant. As recounted in the Holy Quran, particularly in Surah Hud, Surah Al-A'raf, and Surah Al-Ahqaf, Allah unleashed a devastating, barren wind upon them for seven nights and eight days. This fierce tempest annihilated their entire civilization, reducing their grand structures and mighty people to scattered ruins. Prophet Hud and the righteous believers who followed him were miraculously saved by Allah's mercy, serving as a profound lesson on divine justice and the ultimate consequences of rejecting the truth and divine guidance.

Prophet Salih (peace be upon him) was sent by Allah to the ancient people of Thamud, a powerful tribe known for their impressive rock-cut architecture and agricultural prowess, who resided in Al-Hijr, located between Tabuk and Madinah. Despite Allah’s bounties, the Thamud had deviated from monotheism, worshipping idols and living in arrogance. Salih, a noble man from among them, called his people back to the pure worship of Allah, urging them to abandon their polytheistic ways and remember their Creator. The Thamud, skeptical and defiant, demanded a miraculous sign as proof of Salih’s prophethood. They challenged him to bring forth a she-camel from a solid rock. By the will of Allah, a magnificent, pregnant she-camel emerged from the mountain, a clear miracle. Salih conveyed Allah’s command regarding the camel: she was to drink from their water on alternate days, sharing the resource with the tribe, and no harm was to befall her. He warned them that harming her would bring a severe punishment. However, the majority of the Thamud, particularly their arrogant leaders, persisted in their disbelief and rebellion. Conspiring to defy Allah’s command, they hamstrung and killed the blessed she-camel. Salih, grief-stricken, warned them that divine retribution would strike them in three days. True to his warning, on the appointed day, a mighty shriek and an overwhelming earthquake descended upon the Thamud. They were utterly destroyed, found lifeless in their homes, while Salih and the few faithful believers were saved by Allah's mercy. This account is vividly narrated in the Holy Quran, serving as a powerful lesson for humanity.

The Holy Quran presents Prophet Ibrahim, known in Islam as Khalilullah, the Friend of Allah, as a paramount figure of unwavering monotheism and submission. From his youth, Ibrahim profoundly questioned the widespread idol worship of his people, including that practiced by his father, Azar. Through profound contemplation of the cosmos—the stars, moon, and sun—he intellectually discerned the existence of a single, all-powerful Creator, declaring his devotion to Allah, the Lord of all worlds. His steadfast rejection of polytheism led to intense persecution. He famously confronted the idolatry of his community and their tyrannical ruler, Nimrod, resulting in him being cast into a massive fire. By Allah’s miraculous decree, the fire became cool and harmless, sparing his life. Following this, Ibrahim embarked on a sacred migration (Hijra), seeking freedom to worship Allah. He prayed for righteous offspring, and Allah blessed him with two prophetic sons: Ismail through Hajar, and Ishaq through Sarah. His ultimate test came when Allah commanded him to sacrifice Ismail; Ibrahim demonstrated complete submission, ready to obey, before Allah substituted a ram. Subsequently, Ibrahim and Ismail together rebuilt the sacred Kaaba in Makkah, establishing it as the first house of worship for mankind and solidifying Ibrahim's legacy as a guiding light for all who submit to Allah.

christian.text: Abraham, a foundational figure in Christian theology, is introduced in as Abram, called by God from Ur of the Chaldeans. God commanded him to leave his homeland for a land He would show him, making extraordinary promises: to make him a great nation, bless him, make his name great, and through him, bless all families of the earth. This divine call initiated Abraham's profound journey of faith, as he obeyed, trusting in God's unseen plan. Despite his wife Sarah's barrenness and their advanced age, God established a covenant, promising countless descendants and a son, Isaac, through whom the lineage of promise would continue. Abraham's faith reached its zenith when God commanded him to sacrifice Isaac, his only son of promise. Abraham, demonstrating unwavering trust, proceeded with obedience, believing God could even raise the dead. An angel intervened, providing a ram, signifying God's ultimate provision. This narrative, central to the Holy, cemented Abraham's status as the father of faith for both Jews and Christians, lauded in books like and. From his descendants came the nation of Israel, and ultimately, Jesus Christ, the fulfillment of the promise that all nations would find blessing through Abraham's seed.

Demonstrating unwavering obedience, he prepared to fulfill the command, but an angel intervened, and a ram was offered, solidifying his status as a paragon of faith. Abraham lived a life of devotion, purchasing the Cave of Machpelah for Sarah’s and his own burial, establishing the Jewish claim to the land. His legacy endures as the paradigm of monotheism and the progenitor of the chosen nation.

bahai.text: Ibrahim, known as Abraham, stands revered in the Bahá'í Faith as a mighty Manifestation of God, a divine Messenger who inaugurated a significant spiritual dispensation for humanity. Arising in a world steeped in idolatry, Ibrahim courageously proclaimed the oneness of God, calling all to abandon polytheism and embrace true monotheism. His life exemplified profound detachment from worldly attachments and absolute submission to God's will, most notably in his journey from Ur of the Chaldees and the supreme test of sacrificing his beloved son, an act demonstrating a faith unparalleled in its obedience and love for the Divine. Through him, God established a sacred Covenant, promising future blessings and the continuity of divine guidance, ensuring humanity's spiritual progress. Ibrahim's teachings fostered justice, righteousness, and a deeper understanding of humanity's relationship with its Creator. His laid foundational spiritual truths, cultivating a fertile ground for the subsequent Manifestations, including Moses, Christ, Muhammad, and ultimately the Báb and Bahá’u’lláh. The sacred Bahá’í writings, particularly Bahá’u’lláh's **, elucidate Ibrahim's sublime station and the enduring significance of his mission, affirming his pivotal role in the continuous unfolding of God's ever-advancing for humankind.

islamic.text: Prophet Lut (peace be upon him), a nephew of Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham), was chosen by Allah to be a messenger to the people of Sodom. The Quran recounts his mission, highlighting his unwavering call to his community to abandon their grave sins. They were notorious for their unprecedented of indecency, specifically engaging in male homosexuality, which transgressed all natural and divine limits. Lut persistently warned them, urging them to fear Allah, to purify themselves, and to desist from their abominable practices, reminding them of Allah's commands and the purpose of marriage. Despite his sincere efforts and repeated admonitions, his people defiantly rejected his message, mocked him, and threatened to expel him and his family for claiming to be righteous. Overwhelmed by their arrogance and corruption, Lut prayed to Allah for deliverance from their wickedness. Angels, sent by Allah, first visited Ibrahim and then arrived at Lut’s home, bringing news of the impending divine punishment. Lut was commanded to depart with his family before dawn, without looking back. His wife, however, disbelieved and did not heed the command, looking back and suffering the same fate as the transgressors. Allah then utterly destroyed the cities by turning them upside down and raining down stones of baked clay, making their ruins a sign for those who reflect. Lut and his believing daughters were saved, a testament to Allah’s mercy for His obedient servants.

christian.text: Lot, a figure central to several narratives within the Christian, primarily, was the nephew of Abraham. He accompanied his uncle from Ur of the Chaldeans, through Haran, and into Canaan. When their increasing flocks and herds caused strife between their herdsmen, Lot chose to separate from Abraham, settling in the fertile plains of the Jordan, near the wicked cities of Sodom and Gomorrah. He eventually resided within Sodom itself. During his time in Sodom, Lot was captured in battle but subsequently rescued by Abraham. Later, two angels, sent by God, visited Lot in Sodom. Lot showed them hospitality, but the depraved men of Sodom surrounded his house, demanding the strangers for sexual abuse. In a desperate act to protect his guests, Lot offered his own virgin daughters to the mob, an incident reflecting both his desperate plight and the city's extreme wickedness. The angels intervened, blinding the men and revealing God's plan to destroy the cities. Lot was urged to flee with his family. As they escaped, Lot’s wife disobediently looked back at the burning cities and was turned into a pillar of salt. Lot and his two daughters found refuge in a cave. Fearing the loss of their lineage, his daughters made their father drunk on two successive nights and conceived sons by him, who became the ancestors of the Moabites and Ammonites. Despite the complexities of his life and choices, in 2 refers to Lot as a "righteous man, who was distressed by the depraved conduct of the lawless." His story serves as a biblical warning against worldliness and highlights God's judgment on sin.

Lot bravely protected the messengers, even offering his daughters to the mob. The messengers then revealed their purpose, striking the Sodomites with blindness and urging Lot, his wife, and two unmarried daughters to flee, warning them not to look back. islamic.text: Prophet Ismail, a revered figure in Islam, was the firstborn son of Prophet Ibrahim and Hajar. His birth was a divine blessing, and by Allah's command, Ibrahim took Hajar and infant Ismail to a desolate valley that would become the sacred city of Makkah. When Hajar desperately searched for water, the miraculous well of Zamzam gushed forth, sustaining them and eventually drawing people to settle around it. Ismail grew up in this blessed land. A monumental event in his life, recounted in the Holy Quran, was the divine command for Prophet Ibrahim to sacrifice his beloved son. Ismail, displaying unwavering faith and profound submission to Allah's will, encouraged his father to fulfill the command, affirming his complete readiness. Yet, Allah, in His boundless mercy, intervened, replacing Ismail with a ram. This act demonstrated their absolute devotion and served as a lasting lesson for humanity, commemorated annually during Eid al-Adha. Together with his father, Ismail rebuilt the sacred Kaaba in Makkah, establishing it as the first house of worship for mankind. He was also a prophet in his own right, guiding his people towards monotheism and righteous living. Through the noble lineage of Prophet Ismail, the final Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) was born, solidifying Ismail's eternal significance in Islamic history.

christian.text: Ishmael, whose name means "God hears," is a significant figure in, primarily detailed in. He was the firstborn son of Abraham, conceived with Hagar, Sarah's Egyptian servant, at Sarah's request when she was barren. Before his birth, an angel of the Lord appeared to Hagar in the wilderness, promising her a son and foretelling his character: "He will be a wild donkey of a man; his hand will be against everyone and everyone's hand against him, and he will live in hostility toward all his brothers". The angel also promised that her descendants would be too numerous to count. Ishmael was born when Abraham was eighty-six years old. He was circumcised at the age of thirteen as part of God's covenant with Abraham, along with Abraham and every male in his household. Years later, after Isaac's birth, Sarah saw Ishmael mocking Isaac. This led her to demand that Abraham send Hagar and Ishmael away. Though distressed, Abraham complied after God assured him that He would also make a nation of Ishmael because he was Abraham's offspring. God heard the boy's cry in the wilderness of Beersheba, saving them from perishing by revealing a well of water. Ishmael grew up in the wilderness of Paran, becoming an expert archer, and his mother found him a wife from Egypt. records twelve sons, who became tribal chiefs, fulfilling God's promise to make a great nation of him. He was present with Isaac at Abraham's burial.

His mother arranged his marriage to a woman from Egypt. Ishmael had twelve sons who became tribal chiefs, progenitors of twelve nations, fulfilling God's promise of a great lineage. He lived to be 137 years old, and ultimately joined his half-brother Isaac in burying their father Abraham.

islamic.text: Ishaq, known as Isaac, is a revered prophet in Islam, son of Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) and his wife Sarah. His birth was a miraculous glad tiding from Allah. The Holy Quran recounts how angels delivered the astonishing news of his impending birth to his aged parents, who had long desired a child (e.g., Surah Hud 11:71-72, Adh-Dhariyat 51:28-30). This divine intervention beautifully underscored Allah’s power and boundless mercy upon Prophet Ibrahim’s household, fulfilling His promise. Ishaq was divinely chosen, bestowed with prophethood, wisdom, and blessings, thus continuing the noble lineage of monotheism. The Quran consistently affirms his status among the righteous, chosen, and guided servants of Allah. He is frequently mentioned alongside his father, Ibrahim, and his revered son, Prophet Ya'qub (Jacob), as a recipient of Allah’s profound grace and a guiding light. Through Ishaq, the prophetic line extended significantly, and he became the patriarch of a distinguished branch of prophets. His life exemplifies unwavering faith, patience, and complete submission to Allah, serving as an enduring model for believers. Islam regards Ishaq as a pivotal figure in the unbroken chain of Allah's messengers, highlighting the eternal continuity of divine guidance throughout history.

christian.text: Isaac, whose name means "laughter," is a pivotal patriarch in the Christian, primarily recounted in. Born miraculously to Abraham and Sarah in their old age, his birth fulfilled God's promise that Abraham would be the father of many nations. He was circumcised on the eighth day, a sign of the covenant. One of the most profound events of his life was God's command to Abraham to offer Isaac as a sacrifice on Mount Moriah. Isaac willingly carried the wood for his own sacrifice, a moment seen by Christians as a powerful foreshadowing of Christ's sacrifice. An angel of the Lord intervened, stopping Abraham and providing a ram instead, reaffirming God's promise. Later, Isaac married Rebekah, chosen by Abraham's servant. After twenty years of barrenness, Isaac prayed, and Rebekah conceived twins, Esau and Jacob. God revealed that the older would serve the younger. Isaac, favoring Esau, was later deceived by Rebekah and Jacob into giving the patriarchal blessing to Jacob. He experienced God's presence and reaffirmation of the Abrahamic covenant during his life, particularly during his time in Gerar where God blessed him abundantly despite conflict over wells. Isaac lived to be 180 years old and was buried with his parents in the cave of Machpelah, a testament to God's faithfulness across generations.

jewish.text: Isaac, known in Hebrew as Yitzchak, stands as a pivotal patriarch in Jewish tradition, with his life meticulously detailed within in the sacred. His birth was a divine miracle, promised by God to Abraham, then 100, and Sarah, 90, bringing forth laughter, from which his name derives. Circumcised on the eighth day as commanded, Isaac's unwavering faith and obedience were supremely tested during the Akedah, the Binding. Here, as a young man, he willingly accompanied his father Abraham up Mount Moriah, prepared to be offered as a sacrifice by divine command, only to be spared by an angel of God. This profound event solidified his unique role in the eternal covenant. He later married Rebekah, a kinswoman, and after years of barrenness, they were blessed with twin sons, Jacob and Esau. Isaac was the first of the patriarchs to be born in the Land of Israel and notably, he never departed from it, establishing his household there and redigging wells of his father Abraham. Though he faced famine, God commanded him to remain in the land, promising blessings. In his advanced age, blind, Isaac mistakenly bestowed the primary blessing of the birthright upon Jacob, believing him to be Esau, an event that profoundly shaped the future lineage and destiny of the Jewish people. Isaac, who lived to 180, remains an enduring symbol of devotion and a vital link in the unbroken chain of God’s covenant with Israel, buried ultimately in the Cave of Machpelah alongside his revered parents.

islamic.text: Prophet Yaqub, revered in Islam, was a noble prophet of Allah, the son of Prophet Ishaq and grandson of Prophet Ibrahim, all blessed with divine. He is also known by the title Isra'il, from whom the Children of Israel (Bani Isra'il) descended, inheriting a lineage of prophecy and guidance. Yaqub was blessed with twelve sons, among whom was the distinguished Prophet Yusuf. His life was marked by profound devotion and immense patience in the face of severe trials. The most significant trial for Prophet Yaqub was the long and painful separation from his beloved son Yusuf, who was cast into a well by his jealous brothers. Despite the immense grief and the loss of his eyesight from weeping, Yaqub never despaired of Allah's mercy. He maintained unwavering trust and patience, constantly affirming his belief in Allah's wisdom and ultimate plan. The Quran details his steadfastness, particularly in Surat Yusuf, highlighting his beautiful patience and submission to Allah's will. Ultimately, Allah reunited Yaqub with Yusuf in Egypt, restoring his sight and bringing immense joy to his family. Prophet Yaqub consistently preached the pure monotheism of Allah, guiding his family and community to worship the One True God. His final counsel to his sons was to remain steadfast in Islam, emphasizing that they must die as Muslims, fully submitting to Allah. He stands as an embodiment of faith, patience, and profound trust in the divine decree.

christian.text: Jacob, a pivotal patriarch in the Christian faith, was the son of Isaac and Rebekah, and the younger twin brother of Esau. His extensive life story is primarily chronicled in within the Holy. Jacob famously acquired his elder brother Esau’s birthright for a modest meal and, with his mother Rebekah’s assistance, deceitfully obtained the patriarchal blessing intended for Esau from their blind father, Isaac. Fleeing Esau’s anger, Jacob journeyed to Haran. During his travels, he encountered a divine vision at Bethel, known as Jacob's Ladder, where God reiterated the covenant first made with Abraham and Isaac. In Haran, Jacob labored for his uncle Laban, marrying Leah and Rachel, and fathering twelve sons who would become the progenitors of the twelve tribes of Israel, along with a daughter. Upon his return to Canaan, Jacob wrestled with a mysterious figure, often understood as an angel or God, at Peniel, resulting in his name being changed to Israel, meaning "he struggles with God." He subsequently reconciled with Esau. Later in life, during a severe famine, Jacob moved his family to Egypt, where he was reunited with his long-lost son Joseph. Jacob died in Egypt but, according to his wishes and God's promise, his body was carried back to Canaan for burial in the Cave of Machpelah with his ancestors.

jewish.text: Jacob, known in Hebrew as Yaakov, a revered patriarch of the Jewish people, was the son of Isaac and Rebekah, and grandson of Abraham, as recounted in the sacred text of. Born grasping the heel of his twin brother Esau, he later acquired Esau's birthright and received his father's blessing through his mother’s guidance. Fleeing Esau’s wrath, Jacob journeyed to Haran, where he famously dreamt of a ladder reaching to heaven, with angels ascending and descending, and God promising him the land and numerous descendants. In Haran, he served Laban, marrying Leah and Rachel, and fathered twelve sons—the progenitors of the twelve tribes of Israel—and a daughter, Dinah. Returning to Canaan, Jacob wrestled with a divine being at the Jabbok ford, sustaining a hip injury. It was there he was renamed Israel (Yisrael), meaning "he who struggles with God," because he had striven with God and men and prevailed. He reconciled with Esau and settled in the land. Jacob’s deep affection for his son Joseph led to family discord, culminating in Joseph’s sale into slavery. Years later, during a famine, Jacob and his entire household descended to Egypt, reuniting with Joseph. Before his death in Egypt, Jacob blessed each of his sons. His body was returned to Canaan and interred in the Cave of Machpelah, the ancestral burial ground.

islamic.text: Prophet Yusuf, peace be upon him, a beloved son of Prophet Yaqub, was blessed from a young age with divine wisdom. This was revealed through a dream where eleven stars, the sun, and the moon prostrated to him, signifying his future eminence. His jealous brothers conspired against him, casting him into a deep well. He was then rescued by travelers and sold into slavery in Egypt. Yusuf's unwavering faith was tested in the house of Al-Aziz. When his master's wife attempted to seduce him, he resisted, choosing imprisonment over sin. In prison, his prophetic gift for interpreting dreams became evident as he deciphered the visions of fellow inmates. Later, his accurate interpretation of the King's dream, foretelling years of plenty followed by famine, led to his release and appointment as the ruler over Egypt's storehouses, managing the kingdom's resources. During the famine, his brothers came to Egypt seeking provisions, not recognizing him. Through divinely guided events, Yusuf eventually revealed his identity, extending forgiveness for their past wrongs. His parents and entire family then migrated to Egypt, where they were reunited, witnessing the profound fulfillment of his childhood dream. The Quran, in Surah Yusuf, beautifully narrates his inspiring life story, emphasizing patience, trust in Allah, and ultimate triumph.

christian.text: Joseph, a central figure in, particularly, was the beloved son of Jacob. His father's favoritism, symbolized by a coat of many colors, and prophetic dreams of his family bowing to him, incited his brothers' envy. They conspired against him, ultimately selling him into slavery, and he was brought to Egypt. There, Joseph served in Potiphar's household but was falsely accused by Potiphar's wife and imprisoned. Even in prison, God was with Joseph. He interpreted the dreams of two fellow prisoners, accurately predicting their fates. Later, Pharaoh summoned Joseph to interpret his disturbing dreams of seven fat cows and seven thin cows, and seven full ears of grain and seven withered ears. Joseph revealed these foretold seven years of abundance followed by seven years of severe famine. Impressed by Joseph's wisdom and divine insight, Pharaoh elevated him to second-in-command over all Egypt, entrusting him with preparing for the coming scarcity. When the famine struck, Joseph's management saved Egypt and the surrounding lands. His brothers, driven by hunger, traveled to Egypt seeking food. Joseph recognized them, put them through tests, and eventually revealed his identity, forgiving them for their past wrongs. He brought his entire family, including his father Jacob, to settle in Egypt, where they prospered. Joseph’s life exemplifies God's providential hand, turning evil into good and demonstrating faithfulness through suffering to ultimate exaltation, laying the groundwork for the nation of Israel's growth.

jewish.text: Joseph, son of Jacob and Rachel, was deeply loved by his father, who gifted him a special tunic. His elder brothers, resentful of Jacob's favoritism and Joseph’s prophetic dreams, conspired against him. They cast him into a pit and subsequently sold him into slavery to Midianites, who brought him to Egypt. There, Joseph was purchased by Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh. Despite serving faithfully, he was falsely accused by Potiphar's wife and imprisoned. In prison, Joseph's ability to interpret dreams became apparent, correctly foretelling the fates of Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker. Years later, Pharaoh, troubled by his own dreams, summoned Joseph. Joseph interpreted them as signifying seven years of abundance followed by seven years of severe famine. Impressed, Pharaoh appointed Joseph as viceroy of Egypt, tasking him with preparing for the impending scarcity. Joseph wisely managed the land, storing grain during the years of plenty. When the famine struck, Joseph's brothers journeyed to Egypt seeking food. Joseph recognized them but concealed his identity, testing their character before finally revealing himself. Overcome with emotion, he forgave his brothers, understanding that God had sent him ahead to preserve their family. Jacob and his entire household then relocated to Egypt, settling in Goshen. Joseph sustained his family throughout the remaining years of famine. He lived to see his grandchildren and, before his death, made his brothers promise to carry his bones from Egypt to the Land of Israel when God would eventually redeem them. His story is extensively recounted in, part of.

islamic.text: Prophet Ayyub, a descendant of Prophet Ibrahim, was a righteous and wealthy servant of Allah, blessed with extensive lands, livestock, and numerous children. He was known for his piety, generosity, and constant gratitude, using his immense blessings to aid the poor and needy. To exemplify the unwavering faith of His chosen servants, Allah subjected Ayyub to severe trials. He lost all his wealth, his children perished, and he was afflicted with a debilitating illness that caused his body to decay, driving people away. Throughout these immense losses and suffering, Ayyub remained steadfast, never once complaining or questioning Allah’s decree, but instead, constantly praised and remembered Him, embodying the pinnacle of patience (sabr). After many years of enduring his affliction with unwavering faith, Ayyub humbly turned to his Lord, as mentioned in the noble Quran: "And [mention] Ayyub, when he called to his Lord, 'Indeed, adversity has touched me, and You are the most merciful of the merciful.'" (Quran 21:83). Allah responded to his sincere supplication, commanding him to strike the ground with his foot. From this spot, a spring of cool water gushed forth, which Ayyub drank and bathed in, miraculously restoring his health and strength. Allah not only healed him but also restored his family, granting him more children, and doubling his wealth. Prophet Ayyub stands as an eternal example of ultimate patience, unwavering trust in Allah, and the power of sincere supplication. His story is primarily recounted in Surah Al-Anbiya and Surah Sad of the Quran.

christian.text: , whose story is recounted in, was a righteous man of the land of Uz, blameless and upright, who feared God and shunned evil. He was greatly blessed with a large family and immense wealth. Satan, challenging God, claimed ’s piety was conditional on his prosperity. God permitted Satan to test, initially allowing him to lose all his possessions and his ten children. Despite these devastating losses, worshipped God, declaring, "The Lord gave and the Lord has taken away; may the name of the Lord be praised," and did not sin. Satan was then permitted to afflict with painful sores from head to foot. Even his wife urged him to curse God and die, but refused, asserting, "Shall we accept good from God, and not trouble?" Three friends came to comfort him, but their counsel soon turned to accusation, insisting must have secretly sinned to deserve such suffering., however, maintained his innocence, longing to understand God’s purpose. 's endurance through undeserved suffering serves as a profound testament to faith and a deep exploration of divine sovereignty in Christian tradition.

According to the narrative, Satan, the Accuser, challenged God, asserting that 's righteousness stemmed solely from the blessings he received. Three friends came to comfort him but spent weeks debating with, suggesting his misfortune was a consequence of hidden sins. maintained his innocence, wrestling with the apparent injustice of his suffering but never abandoning his faith. Eventually, God spoke to from a whirlwind, revealing His infinite wisdom and omnipotence, prompting to humble himself and repent for questioning divine ways.
