islamic.text: Prophet Shu'ayb, peace be upon him, was a noble messenger of Allah sent to the people of Madyan and al-Aykah, as narrated in the Holy Quran. His people were known for their grave transgressions: cheating in weights and measures, highway robbery, and spreading corruption in the land, besides their primary sin of idolatry. Shu'ayb, known for his eloquence and wisdom, tirelessly called them to monotheism, urging them to worship Allah alone and to abandon their dishonest practices. He implored them to give full measure and weight, not to defraud people of their goods, and to cease their mischief. Despite his sincere warnings and persistent calls to righteousness, the people of Madyan vehemently rejected Shu'ayb's message. They mocked him, accused him of sorcery, and threatened to expel him and his followers from their city. Shu'ayb reminded them of the fates of previous nations who defied their prophets, but they remained arrogant and unrepentant. Consequently, Allah's divine judgment descended upon them. The Quran describes their destruction by a mighty cry, an earthquake, and the torment of the covering cloud, eradicating the disbelievers. Shu'ayb and those who believed with him were saved by Allah's mercy, witnessing the just retribution for Madyan's obstinacy. His story stands as a powerful reminder of accountability and the consequences of rejecting Allah's commands.

christian.text: Jethro, a significant figure in of the Christian, was a priest of Midian and Moses' father-in-law. Moses encountered Jethro after fleeing Egypt, having killed an Egyptian. He found refuge in Midian, where he helped Jethro's daughters at a well. Jethro, also identified as Reuel in some scriptures, welcomed Moses into his home and eventually gave him his daughter Zipporah in marriage. For many years, Moses lived in Midian, tending Jethro’s flocks, before his divine encounter at the burning bush. After the miraculous of the Israelites from Egypt, Jethro heard of the Lord’s powerful deeds. He journeyed to meet Moses in the wilderness at the mountain of God, bringing Moses’ wife Zipporah and their two sons, Gershom and Eliezer. Jethro rejoiced at all the goodness the Lord had shown Israel, offering burnt offerings and sacrifices to God, declaring, "Now I know that the Lord is greater than all gods." Observing Moses laboriously judging the people all day, Jethro offered crucial counsel: to delegate authority by appointing trustworthy men to serve as over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens. Moses adopted this wise counsel, establishing a more efficient system of justice. Having imparted his wisdom, Jethro then departed from the Israelite camp and returned to his own land. His story is primarily found in.

jewish.text: Jethro, known as Yitro in Hebrew, was the father-in-law of Moses, a righteous gentile, and a priest of Midian according to. Initially called Reuel, he is believed to have adopted the name Jethro, meaning "his abundance," after embracing the worship of the one God. Rabbinic tradition suggests he was a prominent figure who, through his own intellectual journey, abandoned all forms of idolatry, a decision that led to his ostracization by his own people. Moses, fleeing Egypt, found refuge with Jethro after an encounter at a well where he defended Jethro's daughters. Jethro welcomed Moses into his household, offering him hospitality and eventually his daughter Zipporah in marriage. Jethro provided Moses with a home and support during his years in Midian before Moses received his prophetic calling at the burning bush. Following, Jethro journeyed to reunite with Moses and witness the miracles of God. Observing Moses burdened by the immense responsibility of judging the people's disputes, Jethro offered profound counsel, recorded in. He advised Moses to delegate judicial authority, appointing capable men to serve as over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens, thereby establishing a structured legal system. This wise counsel was adopted by Moses, proving instrumental in the governance of the nascent Israelite nation. After partaking in offerings to God with the elders of Israel, Jethro returned to his own land, revered for his wisdom and righteous conversion.

islamic.text: Musa, peace be upon him, is one of the most revered prophets in Islam, whose comprehensive story is extensively detailed in the Holy Quran. Born amidst Pharaoh’s tyranny in Egypt, where male Israelite infants were slain, his mother miraculously placed him in a basket on the Nile, leading him to be found and raised in Pharaoh’s own palace. After inadvertently killing an Egyptian, Musa fled to Midian, where he married and tended sheep for several years. Allah Almighty called Musa to prophethood at the sacred valley of Tuwa, near Mount Tur, where He spoke to him directly without intermediary. Commissioned to confront the oppressive Pharaoh and liberate the Children of Israel (Bani Israel) from bondage, Musa performed profound miracles by Allah’s leave, including his staff transforming into a serpent and his hand radiating light. Despite Pharaoh’s defiance and the subsequent plagues upon Egypt, Allah granted Musa and his people passage across the Red Sea, miraculously parting it, while Pharaoh and his army were drowned. On Mount Tur, Musa received the divine scripture, the Tawrat , inscribed on tablets, as a guidance and light for his people. He faced numerous challenges with the Children of Israel, including their worship of the golden calf in his absence, which he vehemently condemned upon his return. Musa, one of the *ulu al-'azm* (prophets of strong will), tirelessly guided his people through the wilderness, always striving to uphold Allah's commandments. His life, a testament to divine mercy and unwavering faith, continues to inspire believers worldwide, affirming his esteemed status as a messenger chosen by Allah.

christian.text: Moses, a foundational figure in Christian scripture, was born in Egypt while Pharaoh ordered the killing of Israelite male infants. Saved by his mother, he was found in a basket on the Nile and raised in Pharaoh's household. After killing an Egyptian, Moses fled to Midian, where God appeared to him in a burning bush, revealing Himself as "I Am" and commissioning him to deliver the Israelites from slavery. Empowered by God, Moses returned to Egypt, confronting Pharaoh and unleashing ten devastating plagues, culminating in the Passover. This led to the miraculous – the liberation of the Israelites, who then passed through the parted Red Sea, escaping Pharaoh's army. In the wilderness, on Mount Sinai, God gave Moses the Ten Commandments and the entire Law, establishing a covenant with Israel. Moses faithfully guided the people for forty years, witnessing God's provision. Due to a single act of disobedience, he was permitted only to view the Promised Land from Mount Nebo before his death, having completed his divine mission as recorded in within the Holy.

jewish.text: Moses, or Moshe Rabbeinu (Our Teacher Moses), is the most revered prophet in Jewish tradition, through whom the entire was given. Born during the brutal enslavement of the Israelites in Egypt, he was hidden by his mother, Yocheved, and placed in a basket on the Nile, where Pharaoh's daughter discovered and raised him. As an adult, he fled Egypt after killing an abusive taskmaster, finding refuge in Midian, where he married Zipporah. At Mount Horeb (Sinai), God appeared to Moses in a burning bush, commissioning him to lead the Israelites out of bondage. Returning to Egypt, Moses, aided by his brother Aaron, confronted Pharaoh, bringing the Ten Plagues upon Egypt until Pharaoh released the people. Moses then led, miraculously parting the Red Sea for the Israelites to cross. At Mount Sinai, he ascended to receive the Divine Law, encompassing the Ten Commandments and all subsequent mitzvot, directly from God. For forty years, Moses guided the Israelites through the wilderness, building the Mishkan (Tabernacle) as a dwelling for God's presence, and preparing them for entry into the Land of Israel. Though he led them to the very border, he was not permitted to enter the land himself due to an earlier transgression. Before his passing at the age of 120 on Mount Nebo, God showed him the Promised Land, and he designated as his successor. Moses remains the unparalleled prophet, the conduit for God's eternal covenant with the Jewish people.

bahai.text: Musa, known as Moses, stands as one of the most revered Manifestations of God, shining brightly in the sacred lineage of divine educators. Baháʼí teachings affirm His exalted station as a Bearer of a new, specifically commissioned by God to guide humanity and foster the spiritual maturity of humankind. His monumental mission was to deliver the children of Israel from the cruel bondage of Pharaoh in Egypt, leading them towards freedom and spiritual renewal. On Mount Sinai, Musa communed with God, receiving the divine Law, famously including the Ten Commandments, which formed the bedrock of a sacred covenant with His people. stands as the Holy Book revealed through Him, containing His laws and guidance for that specific era. The Dispensation of Musa, while perfect and essential for its time, was a vital stage in the progressive unfolding of God's plan for humanity. His laws, though strict and necessary for the spiritual development of a nascent people, were preparatory, designed to educate and refine. Baháʼu'lláh explains that each Manifestation builds upon the last, perfecting and adapting the divine teachings to the needs of an evolving humanity. Musa's profound impact laid the foundation for future Revelations, paving the way for the coming of Christ, Muhammad, and ultimately, the Báb and Baháʼu'lláh, all part of the same eternal truth, fulfilling God's ancient promises. His life exemplifies divine power and unwavering commitment to God's will.

islamic.text: Harun, peace be upon him, was a noble prophet of Allah and the elder brother of Prophet Musa, peace be upon him. When Allah commanded Musa to go to Pharaoh and invite him to monotheism, Musa beseeched Allah to strengthen him with Harun, citing his brother’s eloquence and seeking him as a minister and helper. Allah, in His infinite wisdom, granted Musa’s prayer, declaring Harun also a prophet and sending them both to Pharaoh to deliver His message to free the Children of Israel. Together, they confronted Pharaoh, presenting Allah’s signs and calling him to the truth, enduring his tyranny and disbelief. During, after Allah saved them from Pharaoh and his army, Musa ascended Mount Tur to receive the Tawrat, entrusting Harun with the leadership of the Children of Israel. In Musa’s absence, Samiri misled the people, tempting them into worshipping a golden calf. Harun courageously admonished them, urging them to remember Allah and warning them against this grave error, but the people threatened him, and he waited for Musa’s return to avoid further division. Upon Musa’s return, he confronted Harun, who explained his valiant efforts. Musa then sought Allah’s forgiveness for both of them. Harun is revered in Islam as a righteous prophet whose story is prominently recounted in the Holy Quran, highlighting his unwavering faith and his vital partnership with Musa in delivering Allah's divine message.

christian.text: Aaron, a foundational figure within the Christian ’s, was the elder brother of Moses, born to Amram and Jochebed of the tribe of Levi. Chosen by God, he served as Moses’ eloquent spokesman, enabling his brother to confront Pharaoh and lead the Israelites out of bondage in Egypt. Together, as chronicled in, they performed mighty signs and wonders, including Aaron’s staff transforming into a serpent and its role in the plagues that afflicted Egypt, culminating in the miraculous parting of the Red Sea. He ascended Mount Sinai with Moses, witnessing the giving of the Law. However, during Moses' prolonged absence, Aaron yielded to the people's pressure and fashioned a golden calf for them to worship, an act of idolatry that brought divine displeasure. Despite this lapse, God later consecrated Aaron and his sons as Israel's first priests, establishing the enduring Aaronic priesthood for ministering in the Tabernacle. His divine appointment was further affirmed when his staff miraculously budded and bore almonds, as recorded in. Aaron faithfully served as High Priest until his death at 123 years old on Mount Hor, where his priestly garments were transferred to his son, Eleazar, fulfilling God's sacred ordinances. His life encapsulates themes of divine calling, human weakness, and God's enduring covenant grace.

jewish.text: Aaron, elder brother of Moses, was born to Amram and Jochebed of the tribe of Levi in Egypt, a narrative central to. Divinely appointed as Moses' spokesman, he bravely confronted Pharaoh, assisting his brother in demanding the Israelites' release and performing the ten plagues that ultimately led to from bondage. Following the miraculous crossing of the Red Sea and at Mount Sinai, Aaron was consecrated by Moses as the first High Priest, the Kohen Gadol, an office he held for forty years in the wilderness. His sacred duties involved ministering in the Tabernacle, offering sacrifices, and providing spiritual guidance to the nascent nation. This divine calling was confirmed when his staff miraculously blossomed, affirming his and his descendants' eternal priesthood, the Kohanim. Though he regrettably succumbed to the people's demands during the sin of the Golden Calf, later tradition emphasizes his remorse and God's ultimate forgiveness. Aaron was widely revered for his character, exemplified by his profound love of peace and his tireless pursuit of reconciliation among the Israelites, a virtue deeply cherished in Jewish teachings. He passed away on Mount Hor at the age of 123, a consequence of his and Moses' actions at the Waters of Meribah. His enduring legacy as the patriarch of the priesthood and a symbol of peace continues to inspire.

islamic.text: Dhul-Kifl is a revered prophet in Islam, honored alongside other messengers of Allah. His name appears twice in the Holy Quran, testifying to his esteemed status and divine appointment. In Surah Al-Anbiya (21:85), Allah mentions him alongside Ismail and Idris, declaring them all "of the patient" (min al-sabirin). Similarly, Surah Sad (38:48) lists him with Ismail and Al-Yasa', affirming that they are "among the best" (min al-akhyar). While the Quran does not extensively detail the specific events of his life, his mission, or the people he was sent to, these profound verses unequivocally establish him as a righteous servant and a prophet chosen by Allah. His inclusion among such distinguished prophets underscores his significant role in conveying Allah's message of monotheism and guiding humanity towards truth and righteousness. The epithet "Dhul-Kifl" itself is often interpreted in Islamic tradition to signify "the possessor of the portion" or "the one who took responsibility," reflecting his profound commitment, steadfastness, and dedication to his prophetic duties. He is remembered for his exemplary patience and unwavering devotion, serving as a model of piety and perseverance for believers. His life, though largely unchronicled in extensive narratives within primary Islamic scriptures, embodies the universal virtues of a true prophet as depicted in the Quran, calling people to the worship of the One God and a life of obedience.

christian.text: , whose name means "God strengthens," was a significant prophet and priest in ancient Israel, son of Buzi. His ministry began around 593 BC, during the Babylonian exile, a pivotal period in Israelite history. God called him to prophecy while he was among the Jewish exiles by the Chebar Canal in Babylon, specifically in the fifth year of King Jehoiachin’s captivity. His divinely appointed mission, as recorded in in the Christian, was primarily to his fellow Jewish captives. 's prophecies encompassed several critical themes. He delivered stark warnings of Jerusalem's impending destruction, emphasizing the consequences of Israel's idolatry and rebellion against God. He often performed dramatic symbolic, such as lying on his side for days, shaving his head and beard, and eating a meager diet, to powerfully illustrate God's coming judgment. Beyond messages of doom, also brought profound messages of hope and future restoration for the scattered people of Israel, including the famous vision of the valley of dry bones, symbolizing God's power to revive a spiritually dead nation. He also prophesied about the judgment of surrounding nations and envisioned a future, glorious new temple and a restored land, culminating in the promise that "the Lord is there." His detailed and often dramatic visions profoundly shaped the understanding of divine judgment and ultimate redemption within the Christian tradition.

jewish.text: , son of Buzi and a member of the priestly lineage (Kohen), emerged as a pivotal prophet during the Babylonian exile. He was among the Jewish elite deported from Judah to Babylonia in 597 BCE alongside King Jehoiachin, settling by the Chebar Canal. Five years into his exile, at the age of thirty, received his initial divine, marked by the profound vision of the Merkavah, the Divine Chariot, a central theme in Jewish mystical thought. His extensive prophetic ministry, documented in within the Nevi'im section of , spanned more than two decades. Through a combination of vivid visions and striking symbolic actions, such as lying on his side for extended periods or shaving his head, delivered God's pronouncements. He steadfastly condemned the widespread idolatry and moral corruption prevalent in Judah, foretelling Jerusalem's impending destruction and the eventual ruin of the Temple, events that tragically materialized in 586 BCE. Despite these severe warnings, also conveyed powerful messages of hope and future redemption. His renowned vision of the Valley of Dry Bones vividly symbolized the eventual spiritual and physical rebirth of the Jewish people and the promise of resurrection. He prophesied the future ingathering of the exiles, the restoration of the Davidic dynasty, and provided a detailed vision for a rebuilt Temple in Jerusalem, underscoring God's enduring covenant, the importance of individual accountability, and ultimate sovereignty over Israel.

islamic.text: Prophet Dawud, a revered figure in Islam, was a prophet and a king chosen by Allah for the Children of Israel. His story is recounted in the Holy Quran, which highlights his profound wisdom, strength, and devotion. As a young man, Dawud distinguished himself by bravely slaying the giant warrior Jalut (Goliath) during a battle led by King Talut, a pivotal event that earned him great renown. Following this victory, Allah bestowed upon Dawud both prophethood and kingship, making him a just ruler and a wise judge. Allah granted him the sacred scripture known as the Zabur , which he recited with a beautiful voice, causing mountains and birds to join him in glorifying Allah. Dawud was also taught by Allah the skill of fashioning coats of mail from iron, a craft that provided immense benefit. His life was marked by intense devotion, including a renowned practice of fasting on alternate days, exemplifying his steadfastness in worship. The Quran also narrates an instance where Dawud, in his role as a judge, sought Allah's forgiveness after a trial, which Allah mercifully granted, demonstrating his humility and Allah's vast mercy. Dawud was the father of Prophet Sulaiman (Solomon), another great prophet and king, and together they represented a lineage of divinely guided leadership and wisdom, leaving an enduring legacy of justice and faith.

christian.text: David, a central figure in the Christian, first appears as a humble shepherd boy from Bethlehem, the youngest son of Jesse. Despite his youth, God chose him, and the prophet anointed him as the future king of Israel. His faith and courage were famously displayed when, armed only with a sling and stones, he defeated the Philistine giant Goliath, asserting God’s power over human might. David later ascended to the throne, uniting the tribes of Israel and establishing Jerusalem as his capital. Though a man "after God's own heart," David was not without sin. His most grievous offense involved committing adultery with Bathsheba and arranging the death of her husband, Uriah. Confronted by the prophet Nathan, David showed deep remorse and profound repentance, a testament to his sincere devotion to God. His life, chronicled primarily in books of, and, illustrates both divine grace and human fallibility. David’s reign was marked by both military success and personal tragedy, yet his express profound trust and worship. Crucially for Christians, David is revered as a direct ancestor of Jesus Christ, the Messiah, fulfilling ancient prophecies of a king from the house of David who would reign forever. He is remembered as a powerful king, a gifted psalmist, and a repentant sinner whose lineage carried the promise of salvation.

jewish.text: David, son of Jesse from the tribe of Judah, emerged from humble beginnings as a shepherd in Bethlehem. Chosen by God, he was secretly anointed by the prophet to be the future king of Israel, as recorded in the Books of within. David first gained renown for his unwavering faith and courage, famously defeating the Philistine giant Goliath with a sling and stone, an act that showcased God's hand guiding a young man against overwhelming odds. After serving King Saul, David faced Saul's jealousy and pursuit but steadfastly refused to harm God's anointed king. Upon Saul's death, David was crowned king over all Israel. He conquered Jerusalem, establishing it as the unified capital and the spiritual center, the City of David, bringing the Ark of the Covenant there. Though a man after God’s own heart, David was not without fault; his sin involving Bathsheba and Uriah brought divine rebuke through the prophet Nathan, to which David responded with profound repentance. His deep piety and expressions of praise are immortalized in many, earning him the title "sweet singer of Israel." David's reign laid the foundation for an eternal dynasty, with God promising that the Messiah would descend from his lineage, ensuring his enduring significance in Jewish tradition.

islamic.text: Prophet Sulayman, son of Prophet Dawud, was a noble prophet and mighty king chosen by Allah, whose remarkable life and extraordinary favors are extensively recounted in the Holy Quran. Allah bestowed upon Sulayman profound wisdom from a young age, distinguishing him as a righteous leader and a just ruler. His unique kingdom was unlike any other, as Allah granted him dominion over the mighty winds, which he could command to travel great distances, and subjugated the jinn to his service. Furthermore, Sulayman possessed the miraculous ability to understand the speech of animals, including birds and even ants. He famously employed the jinn in constructing magnificent edifices, most notably the Bayt al-Maqdis, a sacred house of worship in Jerusalem. A pivotal narrative in his life involves Bilqis, the Queen of Sheba. Informed by a hoopoe bird about her people's sun worship, Sulayman invited her to the worship of Allah alone. Upon her intended visit, he miraculously commanded one of the jinn to transport her grand throne to him in an instant before she arrived, showcasing Allah's immense power through him. Witnessing these signs, Bilqis ultimately embraced Islam. Sulayman's life exemplified humility and unwavering reliance on Allah despite his unparalleled authority. His death was equally unique; he passed away while leaning on his staff, and the jinn continued working, unaware, until a worm ate through his staff, causing him to fall, thus revealing to the jinn their ignorance of the unseen.

christian.text: Solomon, also known as Jedidiah, was the son of King David and Bathsheba, born in Jerusalem. He succeeded his father as king of Israel, inheriting a covenant with God. Early in his reign, when God appeared to him in a dream at Gibeon, Solomon humbly requested an understanding heart to govern his people justly, rather than asking for wealth or long life. God was pleased with his request and granted him unparalleled wisdom, making him the wisest man who ever lived, a gift famously demonstrated in his judgment between two women claiming the same child. Solomon fulfilled his father's dream by building the magnificent First Temple in Jerusalem, a dedicated house for the Lord, a project detailed in the Holy. His reign was characterized by immense peace, prosperity, and a vast empire, and his wisdom became legendary, drawing rulers like the Queen of Sheba who came to witness it firsthand. He is traditionally credited with writing the books of, and, found in the Christian. However, despite his extraordinary blessings, Solomon later strayed from God. He married numerous foreign women who led him to worship their idols, turning his heart away from the Lord. This disobedience ultimately incurred God’s displeasure, leading to the prophecy that his kingdom would be divided after his death, a consequence detailed in the Books of 1 and 2.

jewish.text: Solomon, known as Shlomo in Hebrew, was the son of King David and Bathsheba, chosen by God to succeed his father as king over Israel. His life and reign are extensively documented in the Books of and within. Early in his kingship, Solomon sought wisdom from God, who granted him an understanding heart to discern justice, famously demonstrated in his judgment between two women claiming the same child. This divine gift established his reputation for unparalleled sagacity and understanding. Solomon presided over a period of great peace and prosperity for the united monarchy of Israel. His most enduring legacy was the construction of the First Temple in Jerusalem, a magnificent edifice dedicated to God, fulfilling a vision initiated by his father David. He also built his own grand palace and numerous fortified cities, expanding Israel's infrastructure and influence across the region. His fame for wisdom and wealth attracted rulers from distant lands, including the Queen of Sheba, who traveled to witness his glory and test his intellect. Despite his initial devotion, Solomon’s later life was marred by disobedience; he accumulated many foreign wives who led him astray to worship their deities. He built altars for idols, provoking God's anger and leading to the divine decree that the kingdom would be torn from his son after his death, leaving only a remnant for the house of David.

islamic.text: Prophet Ilyas, a distinguished messenger of Allah, is revered in Islam and mentioned in the Holy Quran, notably in Surah Al-An'am (6:85) and more extensively in Surah As-Saffat (37:123-132). He was divinely appointed to guide a community that had strayed into idolatry, specifically worshipping an idol named Ba'l. With profound wisdom and unwavering conviction, Ilyas earnestly called his people to abandon their polytheistic practices and return to the pure monotheism of Islam, the absolute worship of Allah, the Creator and Sustainer of all existence. He admonished them for forsaking the Best of creators for a lifeless idol, reminding them of Allah's might and their accountability. Ilyas pleaded with his community, "Will you not fear Allah? Do you call upon Ba'l and abandon the Best of creators—Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers?" Despite his clear warnings and profound appeal, the majority of his people stubbornly denied his message and persisted in their disbelief and idol worship. For their defiance, the Quran states that they would face punishment, though Allah spared His chosen servants who believed. Allah bestowed enduring peace upon Ilyas, leaving for him commendation among later generations and recognizing him as one of His believing servants who strive for good. His unwavering dedication to Tawhid serves as an eternal example for all believers.

christian.text: Elijah, a prominent prophet of God in ancient Israel, is extensively chronicled in, primarily in the books of 1 and 2 of the Holy. He ministered during the tumultuous reign of King Ahab and Queen Jezebel, a period marked by widespread idolatry, particularly the worship of Baal. Elijah boldly confronted Ahab, declaring a severe drought as divine judgment for the nation's apostasy. During this famine, God miraculously sustained Elijah, first by ravens, then through a widow in Zarephath whose jar of flour and jug of oil never ran out; he also raised her son from the dead. His most famous act was the dramatic confrontation on Mount Carmel. There, Elijah challenged 450 prophets of Baal to a contest, demonstrating the supremacy of the Lord. When the Baal prophets failed to summon fire, Elijah’s prayer was answered by fire from heaven, consuming his water-soaked sacrifice, revealing God's power and leading the people to repentance. Though he later fled Jezebel’s wrath and felt despair, God met him on Mount Horeb with a "still small voice." Ultimately, Elijah did not experience death but was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind by a chariot of fire, witnessed by his successor Elisha. Christian tradition recognizes Elijah's future role, prophesied to return before the great and dreadful day of the Lord, and he notably appeared with Moses alongside Jesus at the Transfiguration, underscoring his enduring significance.

jewish.text: Elijah, known in Hebrew as Eliyahu, was a towering prophet in ancient Israel during the tumultuous reign of King Ahab and Queen Jezebel in the 9th century BCE. His compelling story is primarily recounted in the books of Melachim  within the Nevi'im (Prophets) section of,. Elijah emerged as a zealous defender of HaShem, the One God of Israel, fiercely opposing the widespread idolatry and the worship of Baal promoted by the royal court. His prophetic mission began with a declaration of a severe drought to Ahab, after which he was miraculously sustained by ravens and later by a widow in Zarephath, whose oil and flour never ran out. He famously resurrected her son. The pinnacle of his public ministry was the dramatic confrontation on Mount Carmel, where he challenged the 450 prophets of Baal to call down fire from heaven. When only Elijah's sacrifice was consumed by divine fire, the people returned to God, and the Baal prophets were apprehended. Despite his triumph, Elijah fled Jezebel’s wrath to Mount Horeb, where he experienced God's presence not in wind, earthquake, or fire, but in a "still small voice." He then anointed Elisha as his successor. His life concluded not with death, but with a supernatural ascension into heaven, carried by a chariot of fire and horses of fire in a whirlwind. Jewish tradition anticipates Elijah's return before the coming of the Messiah to herald redemption and resolve intricate legal questions, making him a perennial figure of hope and a divine messenger.

islamic.text: Al-Yasa, peace be upon him, is a revered prophet of Allah, mentioned in the Holy Quran alongside other noble messengers. He is recognized for his piety and commitment to divine guidance. The Quran names him twice, in Surah Al-An'am (6:86) and Surah Sad (38:48), where he is exalted among those chosen and favored by Allah above the worlds. In Islamic tradition, Prophet Al-Yasa is widely regarded as the successor to Prophet Ilyas, peace be upon him. After Ilyas's departure, Al-Yasa continued the sacred mission of prophethood among the Children of Israel, striving to guide them back to the pure worship of Allah. He diligently called his people away from polytheism and idol-worship, emphasizing the oneness of Allah and adherence to His commands. His life was characterized by steadfastness, patience, and devotion to his Lord, exemplifying the qualities of a true messenger. Though the Quran does not extensively detail specific events of his life, Islamic narratives portray him as a righteous leader who faced challenges with unwavering faith, dedicated to reforming his community and spreading Allah's truth by divine grace. He remains an honored figure in Islam, a model of submission and steadfastness in conveying Allah's message.

christian.text: Elisha, a prominent prophet in the ancient history of Israel, is extensively chronicled in the Christian, primarily within the books of 1 and 2. His divine calling began while he was plowing, when the prophet Elijah cast his cloak upon him, signifying his anointing as Elijah's successor. Elisha then left his fields to serve Elijah, becoming his attendant. Upon Elijah's miraculous ascension to heaven in a whirlwind, Elisha requested and received a double portion of Elijah's spirit, as evidenced by his subsequent powerful ministry. He immediately demonstrated this anointing by parting the Jordan River with Elijah's fallen cloak. Throughout his prophetic career, Elisha performed numerous miracles, testifying to God's power. These included healing the poisonous waters of Jericho, miraculously providing oil for a destitute widow, raising the Shunammite woman's son from the dead, and healing Naaman, a Syrian commander, of leprosy. He also famously made an axe head float and revealed the Syrian king's plans to the king of Israel, demonstrating his supernatural insight and protection over God's people. Elisha served as a spiritual guide and counselor to and common people, often intervening in military conflicts and daily life with divine wisdom and power. Even after his death and burial, God’s power was demonstrated when a dead man was restored to life upon touching Elisha's bones, affirming his enduring prophetic legacy in the Christian tradition.

jewish.text: Elisha (also known as Al-Yasa), a pivotal prophet in ancient Israel, is extensively detailed in the Books of within. Called by the prophet Elijah, Elisha, originally a farmer, was chosen by divine instruction to succeed Elijah. He faithfully served his master, witnessing Elijah's miraculous ascent to heaven and receiving a "double portion" of his spirit, an anointing that empowered his own prophetic work. Elisha’s ministry was marked by numerous miracles, demonstrating God's power and direct intervention. He immediately parted the Jordan River with Elijah's mantle, echoing his master’s feat. Among his many wonders, Elisha purified the poisoned waters of Jericho, provided oil for a destitute widow to save her sons, and, most famously, restored the life of the Shunammite woman's son. He also healed Naaman, the Syrian commander, of his leprosy. Throughout his service, his divine insight frequently thwarted the plots of Israel’s enemies. Even after his passing, his sanctity was affirmed; a dead man revived upon touching Elisha's bones, a powerful testament to his enduring spiritual legacy within Jewish tradition.

islamic.text: Prophet Yunus, peace be upon him, known in Islam as Yunus ibn Matta, was a revered messenger of Allah, whose account is detailed in the Holy Quran, notably in Surah Yunus and Surah As-Saffat. He was sent to a large community, often identified as the people of Nineveh, to call them to the worship of the One God. However, his people stubbornly rejected his message and persisted in disbelief. Despairing of their conversion, Yunus, without waiting for Allah’s explicit permission, left his community in frustration. He subsequently boarded a ship that soon faced a severe storm. When lots were drawn to identify who should be cast overboard to lighten the load, it fell upon Yunus. He was thrown into the sea and immediately swallowed by a giant fish, known in the Quran as *al-Hut*. In the utter darkness of the fish’s belly, Yunus realized his error and sincerely repented, supplicating Allah with the profound prayer: "There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers." Allah, the Most Merciful, heard his plea. Allah commanded the fish to cast Yunus onto the shore. He emerged weak and ill, and Allah caused a gourd plant to grow over him, providing shade and nourishment for his recovery. Once restored, Yunus returned to his people. To his astonishment, his community had collectively repented in his absence, believing in his message and seeking Allah's forgiveness, thereby averting a decreed punishment. Allah accepted their repentance, demonstrating His immense mercy and the power of sincere turning back to Him, making Yunus’s mission a profound lesson for all believers.

christian.text: , a prophet in ancient Israel, is prominently featured in book bearing his name,. Called by God to preach repentance to the wicked city of Nineveh, initially disobeyed, choosing instead to flee by ship towards Tarshish. A great storm arose, and believing to be the cause, the sailors reluctantly cast him overboard. The Lord then appointed a great fish to swallow, where he remained for three days and three nights. From within the fish's belly, prayed to God, expressing repentance and faith. Consequently, the fish vomited him onto dry land. God reiterated His command, and journeyed to Nineveh, proclaiming its impending destruction. To 's surprise and displeasure, the people of Nineveh, from the king to the common person, repented in sackcloth and ashes. Seeing their genuine remorse, God relented from the judgment He had threatened., angered by God's mercy towards Nineveh, withdrew. God then used a plant, a worm, and the scorching sun to teach a profound lesson about compassion and His boundless mercy, extending even to those outside Israel. Jesus Himself referenced ’s three days and three nights in the fish as a prefigurement of His own resurrection, and Nineveh's repentance as a judgment against those who refused to believe in Him, as recorded in Gospels.

jewish.text: The prophet (Yonah ben Amittai), whose narrative is recounted in the biblical, was a figure of great significance in Jewish tradition. Commissioned by God to deliver a prophecy of destruction to the wicked city of Nineveh, initially resisted, attempting to flee God's presence by sailing to Tarshish. A great storm ensued, leading the sailors to cast into the turbulent waters as the cause of their misfortune. God then appointed a "dag gadol" (great fish) to swallow, where he remained for three days and three nights, praying fervently from within its belly. Upon 's repentance, the fish vomited him onto dry land. Once more, God instructed to go to Nineveh. This time, he obeyed, proclaiming the city's impending doom. To 's surprise and dismay, the king and all the people of Nineveh responded with sincere repentance, fasting and turning from their evil ways. Witnessing their genuine change, God relented and spared the city., distraught by God's compassion for Nineveh, expressed his unhappiness. God then used a rapid-growing plant, and subsequently a worm and a scorching wind, to teach a profound lesson about divine mercy and the vastness of God's compassion for all creation, including those outside of Israel. His story serves as a central text for reflection on Yom Kippur.

islamic.text: Prophet Zakariya, a revered figure in Islam, was a noble prophet and priest, a descendant of the Prophet Dawud. His account is prominently featured in the Holy Quran, particularly in Surah Al-Imran and Surah Maryam. He was the guardian of Maryam, mother of Isa (Jesus), and marvelled at the miraculous provisions he found with her. Zakariya and his wife, Ishaa', were advanced in age and childless, a condition that deeply concerned him regarding the continuation of his prophetic lineage and the care of the temple. Feeling the weight of his responsibility and fearing what would become of his mission after him, Zakariya earnestly supplicated to Allah for a righteous heir who would inherit from him and the family of Ya'qub (Jacob). Allah, the All-Hearing, answered his heartfelt prayer. While he was standing in prayer in the mihrab, the angels gave him the glad tidings of a son named Yahya , who would be a prophet and confirm a word from Allah. Astonished, Zakariya requested a sign to confirm this extraordinary news. Allah’s sign was that he would be unable to speak to people for three days and nights, except by gestures, despite being otherwise healthy. He then emerged from the prayer chamber, gesturing to his people to glorify Allah morning and evening. In due time, his wife conceived, and Yahya was born, a blessed child to whom Allah granted wisdom in his youth. Zakariya’s story remains a powerful reminder of Allah’s boundless power and His responsiveness to sincere supplication, demonstrating that nothing is impossible for Him.

christian.text: , a venerable priest of the division of Abijah, lived with his wife Elizabeth in Judea. Both were righteous before God, diligently observing all the Lord's commands and decrees, though they were advanced in years and Elizabeth was barren. While was serving in the temple, performing his priestly duties by offering incense, the angel Gabriel appeared to him. Gabriel announced that Elizabeth would miraculously conceive a son, whom they were to name, and that he would be great in the sight of the Lord, preparing the way for the Messiah. Overwhelmed with doubt due to their old age, questioned the angel's words. As a consequence for his disbelief, Gabriel declared that would be unable to speak until the day these things happened. Indeed, he remained mute until 's birth. When the child was eight days old, still silent, wrote on a tablet, "His name is." Immediately, his mouth opened, and his tongue was loosed, and he began to speak, praising God. Filled with the Holy Spirit, then uttered a profound prophecy, recorded in the Gospel of ( of ), known as the Benedictus, which glorified God and foretold ’s crucial role as a prophet who would prepare the way for the Lord and announce salvation through the forgiveness of sins.

jewish.text: , whose name means "God has remembered," was a prominent prophet active in Judah during the early Second Temple period, roughly between 520 and 518 BCE, following the return of the Jewish exiles from Babylon. Identified as the son of Berechiah, son of Iddo, from a priestly lineage, his profound prophecies are compiled in, an integral part of Nevi'im (Prophets) within. Along with his contemporary, the prophet, was instrumental in galvanizing the community, led by Governor Zerubbabel and High Priest, to overcome despair and diligently recommence the rebuilding of the Second Temple in Jerusalem, which had stalled. His prophetic messages were often delivered through a series of eight vivid night visions, interpreted by an angel, which painted a picture of future redemption and the restoration of God's divine presence among His people. ’s profound prophecies detail the coming of the Messiah, the ultimate ingathering of the scattered exiles, the final cataclysmic battles preceding the Messianic era, and the universal acknowledgment of God’s sovereignty. He tirelessly emphasized the imperative of truth, justice, and righteousness, proclaiming that God desires not merely ritual observance but a sincere heart and ethical conduct, as expressed in his call to "execute true judgment, and show mercy and compassion every man to his brother." The book concludes with vivid depictions of Jerusalem's future glory and humanity’s ultimate state under divine rule, offering enduring hope and guidance.

islamic.text: Prophet Yahya, peace be upon him, is a revered figure in Islam, known as the miraculously born son of Prophet Zakariyya and his previously barren wife. The Quran, particularly in Surah Maryam and Surah Al-Imran, details how angels announced Yahya's birth to Zakariyya while he was praying in the mihrab, a profound divine gift signifying Allah's boundless power and mercy in granting offspring in old age. His name, Yahya, was divinely chosen, unique and never before given, marking his special status and the significance of his life. From a young age, Allah blessed Yahya with wisdom, understanding, and prophethood. The Quran describes him as "honorable, chaste, and a prophet from among the righteous," endowed with an intense love for Allah and deep fear of Him. Yahya lived a life of profound piety, asceticism, and detachment from worldly distractions, dedicated entirely to Allah's service. He was celebrated for his kindness towards his parents, his gentle nature, and his freedom from arrogance and rebellion. His mission was to call people to the pure worship of Allah, emphasizing repentance and righteous conduct. He served as a crucial forerunner to Prophet Isa (Jesus), confirming Isa's divine message and preparing the hearts of the people. Prophet Yahya exemplified unwavering adherence to divine truth, ultimately attaining martyrdom for fearlessly upholding Allah's commands against injustice. His life remains a beacon of righteous conduct and submission to the Almighty.

christian.text: the Baptist, recognized in the Christian tradition as the divinely appointed forerunner to Jesus Christ, has his life extensively chronicled in the Gospels of the Christian. His miraculous birth to the elderly priest and his wife Elizabeth, a kinswoman of Mary, was announced by the angel Gabriel, who declared he would be filled with the Holy Spirit from birth and prepare a people for the Lord. lived an austere life in the wilderness, dressed in camel hair with a leather belt and sustained by locusts and wild honey, embodying the ancient prophets. He emerged preaching a powerful message of repentance, urging people to turn from their sins and be baptized in the Jordan River in anticipation of the Messiah. humbly proclaimed himself merely a voice crying out, announcing the imminent arrival of one greater than he, who would baptize with the Holy Spirit. His pivotal moment came when he baptized Jesus in the Jordan, witnessing the Holy Spirit descend like a dove and hearing a voice from heaven affirm Jesus as God’s beloved Son. faithfully testified to Jesus as the "Lamb of God." His unwavering righteousness led him to publicly condemn Herod Antipas’s unlawful marriage to Herodias, resulting in his imprisonment. Ultimately, at Herodias’s vengeful demand, was tragically beheaded, becoming a martyr for his prophetic witness and unwavering truth.

islamic.text: Isa ibn Maryam, known as Jesus, is one of the most revered prophets and messengers in Islam, frequently mentioned in the Holy Quran. His miraculous birth is a profound sign from Allah; he was born to the pure virgin Maryam (Mary) without a father, brought into existence by Allah's command, "Be! And it is!" Even as an infant, Isa spoke to defend his mother’s honor, declaring himself a servant and prophet of Allah, not a divine being or a son of God. He was sent specifically to the Children of Israel, bearing the Injil (Gospel), which affirmed previous scriptures like the Tawrah , and called humanity to worship the One True God, Allah, in absolute monotheism. By Allah’s express permission, Isa performed numerous astounding miracles: he healed the sick, gave sight to the blind, cured lepers, and even raised the dead. He also fashioned birds from clay, breathing life into them by divine decree. Isa foretold the coming of the final prophet, Muhammad, after him. Islamic belief firmly states that Isa was not crucified. Instead, Allah raised him bodily to Himself before his enemies could apprehend him, making a likeness appear to them. Isa remains alive and will return to Earth before the Day of Judgment to establish justice, refute falsehood, break the cross, and ultimately die a natural death before his resurrection.

christian.text: Jesus, central to the Christian faith, is revered as the Son of God and the Messiah, prophesied in. His life, detailed in Gospels (,, and ) of the Holy, begins with his miraculous birth in Bethlehem to the Virgin Mary, conceived by the Holy Spirit. Fully God and fully man, Jesus lived a sinless life, demonstrating perfect obedience to God. His public ministry was marked by profound teachings, parables, miraculous healings, and casting out demons, all revealing God's love and power. He called humanity to repentance and faith in the Kingdom of God. Ultimately, Jesus willingly offered himself as a sacrifice for the sins of the world, suffering crucifixion under Pontius Pilate in Jerusalem. Christian belief affirms that on the third day, God raised him from the dead, triumphing over sin and death. After appearing to many disciples, Jesus ascended into heaven, where he now sits at the right hand of God the Father. Christians await his promised second coming to judge the living and the dead and establish his eternal kingdom. stands as the inspired word of God, providing the foundational truths of Jesus' life, death, and resurrection, which are essential to salvation.

jewish.text: Isa, more commonly known as Jesus, was a Jewish man who lived in Judea during the period of Roman rule. Later rabbinic writings and historical accounts acknowledge his existence as a figure who gathered followers. During his time, he was understood by some to present himself as the awaited Messiah. However, from a Jewish perspective, the criteria for the Messiah, as meticulously detailed within —, which includes, Prophets, and Writings—were not fulfilled by Isa. Jewish tradition teaches that the Messiah will be a human leader, a descendant of King David, who will rebuild the Holy Temple in Jerusalem, gather all exiled Jews back to the Land of Israel, and usher in a universal era of peace and complete knowledge of God. This messianic age is characterized by global adherence to the ethical and legal teachings of, eliminating war and suffering. Isa did not bring about these transformative events, nor did his actions align with the prophetic expectations. Consequently, his claims were rejected by the Jewish religious leadership and the majority of the Jewish people of his era. He was ultimately executed by the Roman authorities, likely for perceived sedition or claims of kingship which threatened Roman sovereignty. Within Judaism, Isa is not considered a prophet, nor is his death understood to possess any redemptive power. Jewish theology maintains the absolute oneness of God and the everlasting validity of, without requiring a divine intermediary or sacrifice for sin.

bahai.text: Isa, revered in the Baháʼí Faith as a mighty Manifestation of God, appeared in Palestine to inaugurate a new and glorious dispensation of divine guidance. Born of the Virgin Mary through miraculous power, He lived a life of profound purity, selfless devotion, and exemplary servitude. His profound teachings, recorded in the Gospels (Injíl) and elucidated by Baháʼí sacred texts, illuminated the path of love, compassion, justice, and the establishment of the Kingdom of God on earth. Isa proclaimed the oneness of God and the inherent unity of humanity, calling all to spiritual transformation and reconciliation. Through His divinely revealed words and wondrous deeds, He unveiled eternal truths, healed the sick, and offered hope to the despairing, emphasizing the spirit of sacrifice and service. He endured immense suffering and was ultimately crucified, an event affirmed in Baháʼí writings as a profound testament to His mission. Yet, His sacred Spirit ascended, and His advent, along with other Manifestations, prepared humanity for the eventual of Baháʼu'lláh, who fulfills the prophecies of His return and brings to fruition the spiritual springtime initiated by Isa, guiding humanity towards its collective maturity and the establishment of global peace.

islamic.text: Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, was born in Mecca around 570 CE, the Year of the Elephant, an orphan raised by his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib and then his uncle, Abu Talib. From a young age, he was renowned for his truthfulness and integrity, earning the title Al-Amin (the Trustworthy). At the age of forty, in the Cave of Hira, the Angel Jibreel (Gabriel) delivered the first from Allah to him, marking the commencement of the Holy Quran's and his divine prophethood. He dedicated his life to conveying Allah's message of pure monotheism, calling people to submit to the One God. Facing severe persecution and opposition from the Meccan polytheists, he migrated with his followers to Medina in 622 CE, an epochal event known as the Hijra, where he established the first Muslim community and a just society governed by divine law. Through wisdom and leadership, he unified numerous tribes, guided his community through defensive struggles, and eventually returned to Mecca triumphantly. He completed his mission, delivering the final verses of the Quran during his Farewell Pilgrimage. Prophet Muhammad, the Seal of the Prophets and the last messenger sent by Allah to all of humanity, passed away in Medina in 632 CE, leaving behind the Quran and his Sunnah (teachings and example) as a perfect guide for mankind.

bahai.text: Muhammad, the Prophet of Islam, is revered in the Bahá'í Faith as a divinely appointed Manifestation of God, a luminary in the eternal succession of Divine Messengers sent to humanity. He appeared in seventh-century Arabia, a land then steeped in idolatry and moral decline, to rekindle the light of God's oneness and establish a new spiritual dispensation. Through Him, God revealed the Holy Qur’án, a sacred scripture containing laws, spiritual truths, and prophecies that profoundly reshaped human understanding and governance. He called people to worship the one true God, advocating for justice, charity, and brotherhood, and to abandon polytheism and corrupt practices. His profound message was met with fierce opposition from those attached to old traditions, leading to His historic migration, known as the Hijra, from Mecca to Medina. There, He established a nascent community dedicated to God’s teachings, demonstrating principles of unity and spiritual governance. Despite immense challenges and persecution, Muhammad tirelessly proclaimed the divine message, inspiring a profound transformation that unified disparate tribes under the banner of Islam and laid the foundations for a vast civilization. Bahá'ís recognize Him as a vital link in the unbroken chain of Progressive, perfectly fulfilling the prophecies of previous dispensations and preparing the way for future Manifestations, including the Báb and Bahá’u’lláh, all reflecting the one indivisible Light of God.
